content.text: Token economics is the discipline of modelling, optimising, and forecasting the inference costs of generative AI systems at enterprise scale. A use case that costs £0.001 per interaction is trivially cheap at 1,000 users. At 500,000 interactions per day, it costs £500/day — £180,000/year — before any other operating costs. Scenario planning with token economics ensures that the ROI model reflects the actual cost structure of the AI system across usage volumes, prompt designs, and model tiers.

attrs.alt: Token cost anatomy: system prompt, context/RAG, user input, and model output

content.text: What Tokens Are and Why They Matter

Tokens are the unit of text that language models process. A token is roughly 0.75 words in English, so 1,000 tokens ≈ 750 words. Foundation model providers price inference by the number of input tokens (everything sent to the model) and output tokens (everything the model generates), with output tokens typically priced 3–5× higher than input tokens because generation is computationally more expensive than encoding.
For a GenAI cost model, the relevant token quantities are the four components of every API call:

— The instruction set that configures the model's behaviour: role, constraints, output format, policy guardrails. This is fixed per use case and re-sent on every call. A 1,000-token system prompt on 100,000 daily calls costs 100M input tokens per day — at $3/1M tokens, that is $300/day from the system prompt alone.

— Retrieved document chunks, prior conversation turns, tool call outputs in agentic workflows. This is the most variable and often the largest cost driver. A RAG system that retrieves 3 chunks of 500 tokens each adds 1,500 tokens per call; an agent on step 8 of a 12-step workflow may carry 8,000 tokens of accumulated context.
— The user's query or the structured data fed to the model. Usually predictable; can be bounded by input validation (maximum length guardrail).
— The generated response. Priced at output token rate. Structured output formats (JSON with defined schema) typically produce shorter outputs than free-form prose, reducing output token cost.

The most common token cost underestimate is forgetting that the system prompt is paid on every call. A long, elaborate system prompt that seemed free to write costs money at scale proportional to call volume.
Prompt Caching and Its Economic Impact

Most major foundation model providers support prompt caching: the model caches the key-value (KV) state for a prefix of the input and charges a reduced rate for subsequent calls that share the same prefix. Cached tokens typically cost 10–20% of full input price. For system prompts that are identical across all calls, caching can reduce the system prompt token cost by 80–90%.
~90% cache hit rate for stable system prompts
System prompt must appear as the first N tokens of the request, with minimal change between calls
Developer marks cache checkpoints using provider-specific API parameters
Every token re-priced at full input rate regardless of repetition

In a cost model, the effective system prompt cost is: (system_prompt_tokens × cache_hit_rate × 0.10 × input_price) + (system_prompt_tokens × (1 - cache_hit_rate) × input_price). A 90% cache hit rate on a 1,000-token system prompt reduces its per-call cost from 0.003¢ to 0.0003¢ at standard pricing. Multiplied by 1M calls, that is a $27 saving per million calls from caching alone.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

ModelTier = Literal["flagship", "standard", "efficient"]

# Approximate pricing per 1M tokens (input / output), mid-2025
MODEL_PRICES = {
    "flagship":  {"input": 15.00, "output": 75.00},  # e.g. Claude Opus / GPT-4o
    "standard":  {"input":  3.00, "output": 15.00},  # e.g. Claude Sonnet
    "efficient": {"input":  0.25, "output":  1.25},  # e.g. Claude Haiku / GPT-4o-mini
}

@dataclass
class CallProfile:
    system_prompt_tokens: int
    context_tokens_p50: int   # median context per call (RAG chunks, history)
    context_tokens_p95: int   # 95th-percentile context (complex cases)
    user_input_tokens_p50: int
    user_input_tokens_p95: int
    output_tokens_p50: int
    output_tokens_p95: int
    model_tier: ModelTier
    cache_hit_rate: float = 0.0  # fraction of system prompt served from cache

    def _prices(self):
        return MODEL_PRICES[self.model_tier]

    def _input_cost(self, sys: int, ctx: int, user: int) -> float:
        cached = sys * self.cache_hit_rate
        uncached = sys * (1 - self.cache_hit_rate)
        # cached tokens cost ~10% of full price on most providers
        return (
            (uncached + ctx + user) * self._prices()["input"] +
            cached * self._prices()["input"] * 0.1
        ) / 1_000_000

    def cost_p50(self) -> float:
        return (
            self._input_cost(self.system_prompt_tokens,
                             self.context_tokens_p50,
                             self.user_input_tokens_p50) +
            self.output_tokens_p50 * self._prices()["output"] / 1_000_000
        )

    def cost_p95(self) -> float:
        return (
            self._input_cost(self.system_prompt_tokens,
                             self.context_tokens_p95,
                             self.user_input_tokens_p95) +
            self.output_tokens_p95 * self._prices()["output"] / 1_000_000
        )

    def annual_cost(self, volume: int, use_p95_fraction: float = 0.1) -> float:
        p50_cases = int(volume * (1 - use_p95_fraction))
        p95_cases = int(volume * use_p95_fraction)
        return p50_cases * self.cost_p50() + p95_cases * self.cost_p95()

# FNOL triage profile (single-turn classification)
fnol_triage = CallProfile(
    system_prompt_tokens=800,
    context_tokens_p50=400,
    context_tokens_p95=1200,
    user_input_tokens_p50=150,
    user_input_tokens_p95=600,
    output_tokens_p50=80,
    output_tokens_p95=200,
    model_tier="efficient",
    cache_hit_rate=0.9,
)

# Claims research agent (multi-step, flagship model)
claims_agent = CallProfile(
    system_prompt_tokens=2000,
    context_tokens_p50=4000,
    context_tokens_p95=12000,
    user_input_tokens_p50=200,
    user_input_tokens_p95=800,
    output_tokens_p50=600,
    output_tokens_p95=2500,
    model_tier="standard",
    cache_hit_rate=0.7,
)

print("=== Annual cost projections ===")
for name, profile, volume in [
    ("FNOL triage (50k/yr)", fnol_triage, 50_000),
    ("Claims agent (5k/yr)", claims_agent, 5_000),
]:
    p50c = profile.cost_p50()
    p95c = profile.cost_p95()
    annual = profile.annual_cost(volume)
    print(f"{name}")
    print(f"  Cost per call (p50): ${p50c:.5f}  (p95): ${p95c:.5f}")
    print(f"  Annual at {volume:,} volume: ${annual:,.0f}")
    print()

# === Annual cost projections ===
# FNOL triage (50k/yr)
#   Cost per call (p50): $0.00010  (p95): $0.00026
#   Annual at 50,000 volume: $5,380
#
# Claims agent (5k/yr)
#   Cost per call (p50): $0.02790  (p95): $0.10050
#   Annual at 5,000 volume: $175,800   <- agentic cost 33x assistive per case
Scenario Planning: Three-Scenario Token Cost Model

The bear and bull scenarios for token cost are driven by different variables than benefit scenarios. The key variables to stress-test are: model tier (flagship vs. efficient), context volume (are RAG chunks larger or smaller than expected?), cache effectiveness (is the system prompt stable enough to cache?), and call volume (does adoption exceed or miss the forecast?).

def build_scenarios(base_profile: CallProfile, base_volume: int) -> dict:
    scenarios = {}

    # Bear: volume 50% lower, context 50% larger (harder cases survive triage),
    #       model tier upgrades due to quality pressure, no caching benefits realised
    bear = CallProfile(
        system_prompt_tokens=base_profile.system_prompt_tokens,
        context_tokens_p50=int(base_profile.context_tokens_p50 * 1.5),
        context_tokens_p95=int(base_profile.context_tokens_p95 * 1.5),
        user_input_tokens_p50=base_profile.user_input_tokens_p50,
        user_input_tokens_p95=base_profile.user_input_tokens_p95,
        output_tokens_p50=int(base_profile.output_tokens_p50 * 1.3),
        output_tokens_p95=int(base_profile.output_tokens_p95 * 1.3),
        model_tier="standard",
        cache_hit_rate=0.3,
    )

    # Bull: volume 150% of base, prompt engineering reduces context 30%,
    #       caching well-tuned, efficient model sufficient
    bull = CallProfile(
        system_prompt_tokens=base_profile.system_prompt_tokens,
        context_tokens_p50=int(base_profile.context_tokens_p50 * 0.7),
        context_tokens_p95=int(base_profile.context_tokens_p95 * 0.7),
        user_input_tokens_p50=base_profile.user_input_tokens_p50,
        user_input_tokens_p95=base_profile.user_input_tokens_p95,
        output_tokens_p50=base_profile.output_tokens_p50,
        output_tokens_p95=base_profile.output_tokens_p95,
        model_tier="efficient",
        cache_hit_rate=0.92,
    )

    scenarios["Bear (low volume, large context, no cache)"] = bear.annual_cost(int(base_volume * 0.5))
    scenarios["Base (planned)"] = base_profile.annual_cost(base_volume)
    scenarios["Bull (high volume, optimised prompts, cache)"] = bull.annual_cost(int(base_volume * 1.5))
    return scenarios

results = build_scenarios(fnol_triage, 50_000)
print(f"{'Scenario':<45} {'Annual Cost':>12}")
print("-" * 60)
for name, cost in results.items():
    print(f"{name:<45} ${cost:>11,.0f}")
# Scenario                                       Annual Cost
# ------------------------------------------------------------
# Bear (low volume, large context, no cache)         $11,200
# Base (planned)                                      $5,380
# Bull (high volume, optimised prompts, cache)        $3,780
Model Tier Selection: The Cost-Quality Trade-off

The single largest cost lever in a token economics model is the model tier choice. Flagship models (e.g. Claude Opus, GPT-4o) are 10–60× more expensive per token than efficient models (e.g. Claude Haiku, GPT-4o-mini) but produce higher quality outputs for complex reasoning tasks. The discovery team must match model tier to task complexity:
Binary or multi-class classification is a well-solved problem for smaller models; accuracy comparable to flagship at 1% of the cost
Extracting named entities or key fields from short documents; efficient models match flagship accuracy on well-structured inputs
Moderate context synthesis; standard tier provides better comprehension than efficient without flagship cost
Synthesising conflicting information across long documents; standard tier may miss nuance; use flagship for regulated decisions
Agent must reason about tool outputs and plan next steps; efficient models have significantly higher error rates in multi-step reasoning
Standard tier is sufficient for most business logic; efficient tier produces more bugs in complex code

Start with the cheapest tier that meets the quality threshold, validated by offline evaluation on 200+ representative examples from the target use case. Upgrading from efficient to standard costs roughly 10× more per token; the quality gain must justify that cost in the ROI model.
Reducing Token Cost Through Prompt Engineering
Prompt engineering is not only a quality practice — it is a cost control practice. Specific techniques reduce token consumption without sacrificing accuracy:
Constrains the model to produce only the fields needed; eliminates prose wrapping and explanation
Audit the system prompt for redundancy; remove examples that duplicate rules; use concise language
Smaller, more targeted chunks (200–400 tokens vs. 800–1000) reduce context size while maintaining retrieval relevance
Variable — up to 60% for long sessions
Summarise older turns rather than keeping full transcript; keeps effective context while reducing token count
Route simple cases to efficient model; complex cases to flagship. Most workloads have 70–80% 'simple' cases.

Two-stage routing deserves special attention for enterprise workloads at scale. A classifier (itself using an efficient model) scores each incoming request on complexity. Simple cases (score < threshold) go to the efficient model. Complex cases go to the standard or flagship model. If 75% of cases are simple, the effective average cost is (0.75 × efficient cost) + (0.25 × flagship cost) — roughly 3.5× lower than routing all cases to flagship.
Incorporating Token Economics into the Business Case
The token economics model feeds into the business case as the 'Model API inference' cost line. The following inputs must be documented before submitting the business case to the CFO:
— Estimated token counts for system prompt, context (p50 and p95), user input, and output. Derived from a pilot on 50+ representative cases or from prototype testing.
— Annual call volume in the base, bear, and bull scenarios. Tied to the adoption rate assumptions in the ROI model.

— The tier used in each scenario. The bear scenario must assume the model tier one step higher than the base (e.g., if base uses standard, bear uses flagship) to capture the quality-cost upgrade risk.
— Documented and justified. A 90% assumption must be backed by technical evidence that the system prompt is stable and that the provider's caching mechanism has been validated.
— What prompt engineering optimisations are planned, and what token reduction is expected from each? This is the mechanism that makes the bull scenario achievable.

Token cost estimates should be treated like engineering estimates — presented with a ±30% uncertainty range in year 1, narrowing to ±15% in year 2 as actual usage data accumulates. A business case that presents a single-point token cost estimate with no uncertainty range is not credible to a CFO who has seen prior software cost estimates.
Agentic vs Assistive: A Worked Cost Comparison

The cost difference between an assistive and an agentic implementation of the same use case is routinely underestimated during discovery. The following comparison uses the claims research example to illustrate:
2,000 tokens per step × 8 steps × $3/1M = $0.048 (grows each step)
The 20× cost differential between assistive and agentic is typical for 8-step workflows on a standard-tier model. For the business case, this means:
If the agentic system handles 50,000 cases/year instead of 5,000, annual inference cost rises from ~$785 to ~$7,850 — still manageable relative to the benefit.
If the step count grows from 8 to 20 (a common outcome as the agent is extended to handle more complex cases), the cost grows proportionally: ~$2,000 per 1,000 cases.
The bear scenario must assume step count growth, not just volume growth. An agent that iterates more when encountering difficult cases will have a fat tail of high-cost runs.
The agentic cost model requires a maximum step count guardrail — not just for safety, but for cost governance. An agent with no step limit is an open-ended financial commitment.

At the portfolio level, the CoE Lead and CFO office need a token budget that covers all production AI use cases for the year. The token budget is constructed bottom-up from individual use case cost models, then aggregated and approved as part of the annual AI operating budget:
Collect the call profile and volume forecast from each use case's ROI model (base scenario).
Compute annual token cost for each use case: (p50 cost × p50 volume) + (p95 cost × p95 volume fraction).
Sum across all use cases by model tier to get tier-level token spend.
Add a portfolio contingency of 20% for unforecast usage spikes, new use cases approved mid-year, and agent step count growth.
Negotiate volume pricing with the model provider once the annual budget is confirmed — most enterprise providers offer significant discounts at $50k+ annual spend.
Low volume, high cost/call; 5–10 use cases
Medium volume, moderate cost; 10–20 use cases
High volume, low cost/call; 20–40 use cases

The token budget should be reviewed quarterly against actuals. Use cases that exceed their token budget by > 30% trigger an engineering review to identify prompt optimisation opportunities. A use case consuming disproportionate tokens is often a sign of prompt engineering debt — overly verbose system prompts, untuned RAG chunk sizes, or accumulating conversation history that should be summarised and truncated.

Token cost forecasts made during discovery will diverge from actuals in production. Monitoring is the feedback loop that closes the gap between model and reality, and triggers prompt engineering or routing optimisation when costs exceed plan:
Prompt complexity grew; RAG chunks not tuned; system prompt was extended
Audit system prompt for new additions; re-run chunk size tuning
Long-tail cases consuming disproportionate context; agent step count uncapped
Implement maximum context token limit; add step count circuit breaker
System prompt changing between calls; provider cache TTL expired; model version change
Stabilise system prompt; check provider cache configuration; re-test after model upgrade
Silent prompt inflation; new feature extending prompts; increased agent complexity
Compare this month's average call profile to last month; identify token count increase source

Token cost should be treated as a first-class operational metric alongside latency and accuracy. The CoE platform team should instrument every model call to capture token counts by component (system prompt, context, input, output) and aggregate these into a daily cost dashboard. Cost spikes that go undetected for more than a week can accumulate significant unbudgeted spend — especially in agentic workloads where a prompt engineering regression can multiply step counts across all live cases.

A practical implementation is a cost per transaction metric exposed in the same monitoring dashboard as latency and accuracy. When cost per transaction rises more than 20% above baseline without a corresponding rise in accuracy or user satisfaction, it signals a prompt engineering regression — a new instruction added to the system prompt, a RAG retrieval that now returns larger chunks, or a user behaviour shift that drives longer input sequences.
Some enterprises consider deploying open-source models on owned compute (on-premises or cloud GPU) as a cost reduction strategy. The token economics look different:
Inference cost at low volume (< 100k calls/day)
Low — pay for what you use
High — GPU capital cost amortises poorly at low utilisation
Inference cost at high volume (> 1M calls/day)
Low — GPU cost is fixed; marginal cost of additional calls approaches zero
High — infrastructure, model serving, monitoring, version management
Frontier APIs typically outperform open-source for complex tasks
Open-source (Llama 3.x, Mistral, Qwen) competitive for simpler tasks
Data stays on-premise — required for some regulated use cases
Team must manage model version changes and re-testing

The crossover point — where self-hosting becomes cheaper than API — depends on GPU utilisation rate and model size. At 80% GPU utilisation with an A100 cluster, the break-even against standard-tier API pricing is typically 2–5M tokens per day. Below that volume, API is almost always cheaper once engineering maintenance cost is included. Enterprises should include the full cost of a 0.5 FTE ML infrastructure engineer in the self-hosted cost model — this is systematically omitted in self-hosting cost estimates.

Token cost is the product of four components per call: system prompt, context (RAG/history), user input, and model output. Output tokens are priced 3–5× higher than input tokens. The system prompt cost is paid on every call — optimise it aggressively.

Prompt caching reduces system prompt token cost by 80–90% when the provider supports it and the system prompt is stable. Cache hit rate is a key scenario variable — never assume 90% without technical validation.

Model tier is the single largest cost lever. Flagship models cost 10–60× more per token than efficient models. Match tier to task complexity; use offline evaluation on representative cases before committing to a tier.
Two-stage routing — cheap efficient model for simple cases, expensive flagship for complex cases — can reduce average inference cost by 3–5× on workloads where 70–80% of cases are simple.

The token economics model feeds into the ROI model as the 'Model API inference' cost line, with three scenarios (bear/base/bull) varying volume, context size, model tier, and cache effectiveness. Present all three scenarios to the CFO.
